title: Charlize Theron, Plus an Interview With ‘Long Shot’ Director Jonathan Levine | Career Arc
format: Sean Fennessey, Amanda Dobbins, and Rob Harvilla walk Charlize Theron's career in reverse, from model-decoration bit parts through the Monster Oscar to her action-and-indie reinvention, then review Long Shot. Plus: Sean talks to its director, Jonathan Levine.

segments.heading: The model years
segments.note: the first half-decade, cast as an object

films.title: Two Days in the Valley
films.note: Rob's breakthrough pick. An extremely-90s LA ensemble; her character Helga Svelgen is introduced via a bloody Polaroid and a James Spader sex scene. 'You're not too tall, it's the world that's too short.'

films.title: That Thing You Do!
films.note: Amanda's pick, and the first time she saw Theron: the beautiful, cold hometown girlfriend who dumps the drummer for her dentist, playing vapid and in on the joke.

films.title: The Devil's Advocate
films.note: Sean's breakthrough pick. As Mary Ann Lomax, the one person trying to act for real inside a ludicrous Al Pacino/Keanu Reeves movie, curly blonde to shorn brunette.

films.title: Celebrity
films.note: Minor Woody Allen; her character is literally named Supermodel. Nobody on screen can talk to her like a human.

films.title: The Cider House Rules
films.note: The warm-bath literary adaptation; not the main character.

films.title: Reindeer Games
films.note: Stuck beside Ben Affleck, part of the long stretch subservient to the male lead.

films.title: The Italian Job
films.note: The popcorny heist mode Amanda came to her through.

segments.heading: Monster, and the reinvention
segments.note: the Oscar, then a decade of misfires

films.title: Monster
films.note: Patty Jenkins's Aileen Wuornos. The example everyone cites for uglify-to-win-an-Oscar, but the hosts land on: braver at the time than in memory, and as much for going all the way as for the teeth.

films.title: Head in the Clouds
films.note: One of the new-lead swings that immediately followed the win, and it flopped.

films.title: North Country
films.note: A second Oscar nomination, for a mining-industry sexual-harassment drama that failed at the box office and left little cultural footprint.

films.title: Aeon Flux
films.note: Her bid for action stardom, notoriously recut and mismanaged; a dry run for Atomic Blonde that lost money. On Simmons's podcast she just went 'ugh.'

films.title: In the Valley of Elah
films.note: Paul Haggis's first film after Crash; heavy-handed, with a good Tommy Lee Jones.

films.title: Hancock
films.note: Roughly ten years ahead of Captain Marvel. She does the emotional lifting opposite Will Smith; the twist is obvious in the first ten minutes.

segments.heading: Personal pinnacles, and the action turn
segments.note: each host's peak, plus the 40s reinvention

films.title: Mad Max: Fury Road
films.note: Sean's pinnacle and, the room agrees, her best film. Furiosa is a near-silent, glowering, John Wayne-stoic performance behind the wheel, the image you now picture as new Charlize.

films.title: Young Adult
films.note: Rob's pinnacle. A stunted, misanthropic YA author the movie graciously declines to redeem. It's the tipping point where she reinvented herself just as movie stars stopped mattering.

films.title: Tully
films.note: Amanda's pinnacle. A cool pivot straight off the action run; the middle-hour two-hander with Mackenzie Davis is the best stretch of the movie, even if the ending is too neat.

films.title: Atomic Blonde
films.note: Amanda's favorite. The staircase fight and a genuinely great performance under the confusing triple-cross plot; a raucous South by screening that never quite crossed over.

films.title: Snow White and the Huntsman
films.note: Hamming it up as the evil queen; best remembered now for the Kristen Stewart/Rupert Sanders tabloid moment.

films.title: Prometheus
films.note: Sean defends it; her role is subservient to Guy Pearce's ancient character.

films.title: A Million Ways to Die in the West
films.note: Seth MacFarlane's very bad Western comedy, the 'cool girl' streak at its most ineffective.

films.title: The Fate of the Furious
films.note: As Cypher, a part likely written for a man, seemingly yelling into a microphone at Vin Diesel from a blacked-out room.

films.title: The Addams Family
films.note: Forthcoming then: she voices Morticia in the animated film.

films.title: Bombshell
films.note: Then titled Fair and Balanced, Jay Roach's Fox News drama, with Theron as Megyn Kelly.

films.title: Long Shot
films.note: A crowd-pleasing shade for her: a powerful politician who falls for Seth Rogen's schlub speechwriter. The hosts argue it short-circuits the usual rom-com tropes (she's already funny and human, doesn't need a man to fix her) and is honest about both the chemistry and how striking she is at 40-something. Features a Boyz II Men performance.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.note: Sean and the writer-director on the movie and the state of studio comedy

films.title: Long Shot
films.note: Dan Sterling wrote it during the Obama years; Rogen and Theron produced. Levine reworked it toward 2019, aiming for an old-Hollywood Hepburn/Tracy two-hander with a lighter political touch than Colbert or Vice.

films.title: Warm Bodies
films.note: Shot, like Long Shot, in Montreal; the last script Levine wrote himself, and his own idea of a romantic comedy.
Where he learned the two-camera, improv Apatow style he still uses, and first pushed Rogen toward vulnerability.

films.title: The Night Before
films.note: The prior collaboration where he felt improvisation was crowding out the filmmaking.

films.title: All the Boys Love Mandy Lane
films.note: His debut; sold big out of Toronto, then barely came out. His cautionary tale about festival mythology.

films.title: The Wackness
films.note: Won the Sundance audience award and didn't capitalize the way he'd hoped.

films.title: Funny People
films.note: Levine's example of Rogen doing vulnerability outside his own films.

films.title: Steve Jobs
films.note: The 'Jobs movie,' cited as more Rogen dramatic work.

films.title: Knocked Up
films.note: The rom-com Long Shot is measured against; Levine likes that it never labors to prove its woman isn't funny.

films.title: Vice
films.note: Adam McKay's; the pointed-political-comedy model Levine deliberately did not want to make.

films.title: Closer
films.note: He rewatched the Julia Roberts confrontation the morning of Long Shot's breakup scene, for the Mike Nichols blocking.

films.title: When Harry Met Sally...
films.note: One of the sophisticated rom-com touchstones.

films.title: Pat and Mike
films.note: The kind of Hepburn/Tracy movie Levine watches in pre-production but doesn't assign to his actors.

films.title: Game Night
films.note: An Edgar Wright-ish comedy he stumbled into that made him feel good about the genre's future.

films.title: Ingrid Goes West
films.note: How he first heard about O'Shea Jackson Jr.

films.title: Straight Outta Compton
films.note: Where he'd already seen Jackson.

films.title: Us
films.note: The last thing he saw in a theater; loved it, admits he was a little confused.

films: Extremely Wicked, Shockingly Evil and Vile

A Million Ways to Die in the West.title: A Million Ways to Die in the West

All the Boys Love Mandy Lane.title: All the Boys Love Mandy Lane

Extremely Wicked, Shockingly Evil and Vi.title: Extremely Wicked, Shockingly Evil and Vile
